title: 命案目睹记
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\136-命案目睹记-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Claddock questions Joëlleit regarding whether Anna mentioned having a son. Joëlleit finds the idea unlikely, noting that most girls in the troupe use addresses for pregnancy support. Joëlleit also mentions that Anna was not close to the other girls. Claddock subsequently interviews other members of the ballet troupe, who describe Anna as a habitual liar who fabricated stories about being a mistress to dukes or millionaires, a secret resistance worker, or a Hollywood star. One girl suggests Anna’s background was actually a dull middle-class life. Because the body found in a coffin at Rutherford Manor was too swollen for certain identification, the girls remain uncertain if it was Anna. It is confirmed that on December 19th, Anna decided not to return to France, and on December 20th, a woman resembling her took a train to Blakemore, where she was strangled. Claddock considers if Anna is hiding with a man or if she is connected to Edmund Crakenstall.

In Paris, Klaudia and Deson discuss Martina. Deson agrees the matter may be separate from the corpse but intends to search for marriage records between Lieutenant Edmond of the Fourth Regiment and a French girl named Matilda from the time of the Dunkirk evacuation, though wartime destruction may have erased such files.

Back at the station, Weatherall reports that no one at 136 Cressida Avenue can vouch for Martina’s identity, as it is a student apartment. Investigations at local hotels show no record of Martina Crikenthorpe or Anna Stramwinsky, though Anna checked out of the Brooke Green hotel on December 19th. Claddock then meets with Wimbush, a lawyer, to discuss Martina’s letter. Wimbush expresses anger and skepticism regarding the claims of a marriage and a son, suggesting the letter is an attempt to extort maintenance from the Crickenshaw family.
